## III. Stonemill's Leverage — Consolidated Map

Because the client has identified Stonemill's posture as the controlling variable, we consolidate here every leverage point Stonemill holds across the governance documents. Each is cross-referenced to the detailed analysis in the relevant section.

| # | Leverage Point | Source | Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Series C separate-class veto on any Liquidation Event** | Restated Cert. §4.3.6(b)(i) | **Absolute block.** ≥66⅔% of Series C required; Stonemill holds 100%. Cannot be satisfied without Stonemill. |
| 2 | All-Preferred class vote on Liquidation Event | Restated Cert. §4.3.6(a)(vi) | >50% of Preferred required. Achievable without Stonemill (Ridgeline + Northbridge = 8.7M > 8.25M), but only barely and only if both consent. |
| 3 | Series C separate-class consent on charter amendments adversely/disproportionately affecting Series C | Restated Cert. §4.3.6(b)(ii) | If the Transaction or merger agreement requires a charter amendment, another ≥66⅔% Series C vote — another Stonemill veto. |
| 4 | All-Preferred consent on charter amendments adversely affecting Preferred | Restated Cert. §4.3.6(a)(i), §8.1 | >50% of Preferred; achievable without Stonemill but tight. |
| 5 | Preferred consent on indebtedness > $5M | Restated Cert. §4.3.6(a)(v) | Relevant to the Clearfield term loan (Area 8). If consent was not obtained for the $12M loan, Stonemill has a breach claim — additional leverage. |
| 6 | Board seat (Series C Director) | Voting Ag. §2.2(c) | Julian Trask votes on Board matters; has voted against Hargrove-related actions (exclusivity, definitive-doc authorization). Cannot block Board action alone (1 of 7) but creates a dissent record. |
| 7 | Board observer right | Voting Ag. §4 | Non-voting observer entitled to all Board materials; information access. (Absent from April 17, 2024 meeting — confirm notice.) |
| 8 | Drag-along — cannot be triggered against Stonemill | Stockholders' Ag. §3.1 | Under the broad reading of prong (i), the drag-along cannot be triggered without Stonemill's own votes; under the narrow reading it can, but overriding the charter veto is uncertain. Not a reliable tool against Stonemill. |
| 9 | Economic break-even | Waterfall (Appendix B) | Series C receives only its $18.50 preference (break-even) while all others receive upside — the economic motivation to extract concessions. |
| 10 | Dissent record / process objections | Board minutes (Apr. 17, Jul. 10, Sep. 25, 2024) | Trask's objections and votes create a record Stonemill can use to challenge the sale process (Revlon) or support a blocking posture. |

**Conclusion.** Stonemill's leverage is anchored by the charter-level Series C separate-class veto (item 1), which is absolute and cannot be circumvented by contract. The remaining points (items 2–10) reinforce Stonemill's position and provide additional negotiating chips and litigation ammunition. The practical implication is that **Hargrove must treat Stonemill as a consenting party, not a party to be dragged.**

## IV. Area 1 — Change-of-Control Consent Requirements

### A. Charter Protective Provisions (Restated Certificate)

The Transaction is a "Liquidation Event" under §4.3.2(d). Specifically, §4.3.2(d)(i) captures any merger in which the Company's stockholders do not retain at least 50% of the voting power of the surviving/resulting entity, and §4.3.2(d)(iii) captures any transaction transferring more than 50% of the Company's voting power. The reverse triangular merger satisfies both prongs (Terravox stockholders receive cash and retain 0% of Hargrove). The Company must give Preferred holders at least 20 days' written notice of the Liquidation Event with material terms and a good-faith per-share distribution estimate (§4.3.2(d), last paragraph).

Two protective provisions are triggered:

1. **All-Preferred class vote — §4.3.6(a)(vi).** The Company may not "effect any Liquidation Event" without the prior written consent or affirmative vote of holders of **more than 50% of the then-outstanding Preferred Stock, voting together as a single class on an as-converted basis.** The charter fixes the threshold at **>8,250,000 shares** (based on 16,500,000 Preferred outstanding). **Can be met without Stonemill:** Ridgeline (3,500,000) + Northbridge (5,200,000) = 8,700,000 > 8,250,000 — but only if **both** Ridgeline and Northbridge consent. If either withholds, this vote fails.

2. **Series C separate-class vote — §4.3.6(b)(i).** The Company may not "effect any Liquidation Event" without the prior written consent or affirmative vote of holders of **at least 66⅔% of the then-outstanding Series C Preferred Stock, voting as a separate class.** The charter fixes the threshold at **≥5,200,000 shares** (based on 7,800,000 Series C outstanding). **Cannot be met without Stonemill:** Stonemill holds 100% of the Series C (7,800,000 shares). This is an **absolute veto.** The charter expressly states this consent is "separate from and in addition to" the all-Preferred consent (§4.3.6(b), penultimate paragraph).

### B. DGCL Requirements

Under DGCL §251, the merger requires (i) approval of the Board of Directors and (ii) approval of the holders of a majority of the outstanding shares of stock entitled to vote. The Restated Certificate provides that Common and Preferred vote together as a single class on an as-converted basis (§4.2.1, §4.3.5). Unexercised options/RSUs are not "outstanding shares" and do not vote (subject to confirmation of the equity plan terms — Area 7). On a denominator of outstanding Common plus Preferred-as-converted (~26.3M), the threshold is ~13.15M. **Can be met without Stonemill:** Founders (7.3M) + Ridgeline (3.5M) + Northbridge (5.2M) = 16.0M > 13.15M. ✓

The Bylaws permit stockholder action by written consent in lieu of a meeting (Bylaws §2.6; Restated Cert. §6.2), which can be used to obtain the consents efficiently. Note, however, that the all-Preferred and Series C consents are **separate class** consents that must be independently solicited; they are not satisfied by the single-class DGCL vote.

### C. Stockholders' Agreement

The "Sale of the Company" definition (§1.1) captures the merger (merger in which stockholders do not retain majority of voting power of surviving entity; transaction transferring >50% voting power). The Stockholders' Agreement does not impose a freestanding consent for the merger beyond the drag-along mechanism (Area 2). It **terminates** upon a Sale of the Company (§6.1(b)); see Area 4.

### D. Voting Agreement

The "Sale of the Company" definition (§1) is identical to the Stockholders' Agreement and captures the merger. The Voting Agreement **terminates** upon consummation of a Sale of the Company (§7.1(b)); see Area 5. It imposes no freestanding merger consent.

### E. Registration Rights Agreement

The RRA terminates on a "Deemed Liquidation Event" (§9(c)) — a term the RRA does not define. See Area 10.

### F. Consent Checklist

The consolidated consent/approval matrix is set out in **Appendix A**. In summary, the Transaction requires, at minimum: (1) Board approval of the definitive merger agreement; (2) the DGCL §251 stockholder vote; (3) the all-Preferred class consent (§4.3.6(a)(vi)); and (4) the Series C separate-class consent (§4.3.6(b)(i)). Item (4) is the gating item and requires Stonemill. If the merger agreement or Transaction structure requires a charter amendment, additional consents under §4.3.6(a)(i) and §4.3.6(b)(ii) may be triggered (another Stonemill veto).

## V. Area 2 — Drag-Along Analysis

### A. The Three-Pronged Trigger (Stockholders' Ag. §3.1(a))

A "Drag-Along Approval" requires the satisfaction of **all three** of the following (§3.1(b) is explicit that fewer than all three does not constitute a Drag-Along Approval):

1. **Prong (i):** holders of at least a majority of the outstanding shares of Common Stock, calculated on an as-converted basis (treating each share of Preferred as converted);
2. **Prong (ii):** holders of at least a majority of the outstanding Preferred Stock, as-converted, voting as a single class; and
3. **Prong (iii):** at least two (2) of the three (3) Investor Stockholders.

### B. Can the Drag-Along Be Triggered Without Stonemill?

**Prong (ii):** >50% of 16.5M Preferred = >8.25M. Ridgeline (3.5M) + Northbridge (5.2M) = 8.7M > 8.25M. **Satisfied without Stonemill** (barely, and only if both consent). ✓

**Prong (iii):** 2 of 3 Investors. Ridgeline + Northbridge = 2 of 3. **Satisfied without Stonemill.** ✓

**Prong (i):** This is the dispositive question, and it turns on an **ambiguous denominator.** The provision reads: "a majority of the outstanding shares of Common Stock (calculated on an as-converted to Common Stock basis, treating each share of Preferred Stock as having been converted...)." Two readings:

- **Narrow reading (options excluded):** "Outstanding shares of Common Stock" means issued and outstanding common shares; the parenthetical adds Preferred-as-converted but is silent on options. Denominator ≈ outstanding Common (~9.8M) + Preferred-as-converted (16.5M) ≈ **26.3M**. Threshold ≈ >13.15M. Founders (7.3M) + Ridgeline (3.5M) + Northbridge (5.2M) = **16.0M > 13.15M. Satisfied without Stonemill.** ✓
- **Broad reading (options included):** "As-converted basis" is read to include all common-equivalent shares, i.e., the full 33.9M fully-diluted count. Denominator ≈ **33.9M**. Threshold ≈ >16.95M. Founders + Ridgeline + Northbridge = **16.0M < 16.95M. NOT satisfied without Stonemill** (short by ~950,000 shares), unless additional common/option holders vote in favor. ✗

**The better reading is the narrow one** (options excluded), because the parenthetical specifies only Preferred conversion and does not mention option exercise — had the drafters intended to include options, they would have said so (as the Stockholders' Agreement elsewhere does, e.g., Exhibit B's fully-diluted definition expressly includes options). However, the broad reading is colorable, and Stonemill would advance it. **This ambiguity is itself a Significant risk:** the answer determines whether the drag-along is even available as a tool.

### C. Can the Drag-Along Override the Charter Series C Veto?

Even assuming the drag-along can be triggered without Stonemill (narrow reading), a second, harder question arises: **can a contractual drag-along compel Stonemill to waive its charter-given Series C separate-class consent right?**

Arguments **for** enforceability:
- §3.1(a)(A) obligates each Restricted Stockholder (Stonemill is one) to "vote ... all Shares ... in favor of such Sale of the Company and in favor of any other matter required to be approved in connection with such Sale of the Company" — which on its face includes the Series C separate-class consent.
- §3.3 grants an **irrevocable proxy**, coupled with an interest, to the holders constituting the Drag-Along Approval to vote the dragged stockholder's shares in favor — a mechanism to compel the vote even over objection.
- Delaware generally enforces stockholders'-agreement voting obligations as contracts, including agreements to consent to transactions.

Arguments **against** (charter veto prevails):
- The Series C separate-class vote is a **charter** protective provision — part of the certificate of incorporation, the supreme corporate document. A stockholders' agreement is a contract among stockholders and cannot rewrite the charter.
- The charter does **not** contain a carve-out subjecting the protective provisions to the drag-along (some charters do; this one does not). The drafters' silence is meaningful.
- DGCL §242(b)(2) protects class voting rights in the charter; while §4.3.6(b)(i) governs a Liquidation Event rather than a charter amendment per se, the policy favoring non-waivable charter class protections is strong.
- Fiduciary-duty overlay: a court may decline to enforce a drag-along that would breach fiduciary duties, though a third-party cash deal at a fair price approved by an independent committee reduces this concern.

**Assessment.** This is a genuinely contested question of Delaware law with no controlling on-point authority. The most likely practical outcome is that **Stonemill would litigate** any attempt to drag it, seeking to enjoin the closing on the grounds that (a) the drag-along was not properly triggered (broad reading of prong (i)) and/or (b) the drag-along cannot override the charter veto. The outcome is uncertain, the process would be slow and expensive, and it would jeopardize the Q1 2025 target closing.

### D. Conclusion on the Drag-Along

The drag-along is **not a reliable path to closing over Stonemill's objection.** It is, at best, a contingent and litigable fallback that depends on resolving two independent uncertainties (the prong (i) denominator and the charter-override question) in Hargrove's favor. The client should **not** rely on the drag-along to change the negotiation dynamic. The drag-along's principal value here is as a **negotiating lever** — the threat of an attempt, and the cost and uncertainty Stonemill would face in resisting it — rather than as a sure mechanism. We recommend flagging this clearly to Katrina Voss.

## VI. Area 3 — Impediments to Closing a Reverse Triangular Merger

### A. The Reverse Triangular Structure Is Clearly Captured

The defined terms across the documents clearly capture a reverse triangular merger in which Terravox survives as a wholly-owned subsidiary and its stockholders receive cash:

- **"Liquidation Event"** (Restated Cert. §4.3.2(d)(i) and (iii)): captured — Terravox stockholders retain 0% of Hargrove's voting power, and >50% of Terravox's voting power is transferred to Hargrove.
- **"Sale of the Company"** (Stockholders' Ag. §1.1; Voting Ag. §1): captured — same two prongs.

We considered the possible argument that §4.3.2(d)(i)'s reference to "the surviving or resulting entity" could be read to mean Terravox itself (which technically survives the Merger Sub merger), such that the stockholders' retention of voting power is measured in Terravox. That argument fails: after the merger, Terravox is wholly owned by Hargrove, so the former Terravox stockholders retain 0% of Terravox as well. Either way, the merger is a Liquidation Event. **No structural ambiguity** on this point.

### B. Consequence: The Waterfall Applies

Because the merger is a Liquidation Event, merger consideration is distributed **in accordance with the liquidation waterfall** (Restated Cert. §4.3.2; Stockholders' Ag. §3.2(a), which expressly provides that "consideration shall be distributed in accordance with the liquidation waterfall provisions of the Restated Certificate as if the Sale of the Company were a Liquidation Event"). The waterfall analysis (Appendix B) controls the per-share economics and confirms that Series C receives only its preference. The merger agreement's consideration-allocation mechanics must track this waterfall.

### C. Reverse Triangular Merger — Structural Notes

- **Survival of the entity:** Because Terravox survives as a subsidiary, its legal entity, contracts, licenses, and regulatory approvals continue in form, which is generally favorable for preserving the going-concern business. This does not reduce the consent requirements — the Liquidation Event is triggered at the merger stage regardless of forward vs. reverse structure.
- **Charter/Bylaws post-closing:** Terravox's charter and bylaws technically remain in effect as a subsidiary, but the protective provisions will have been satisfied (or waived) at closing, and Hargrove, as sole stockholder, will control the subsidiary post-closing.
- **Debt repayment:** The $12M Clearfield term loan (secured by substantially all assets, including IP) must be repaid at or before closing; this is reflected in the $473M equity value. Confirm the loan's change-of-control covenant does not trigger an event of default pre-closing (request the loan agreement — see Area 8 / document request list).

### D. Material Error in the Board Minutes

The September 25, 2024 minutes state that "all holders of Preferred Stock would receive amounts in excess of their respective liquidation preferences on an as-converted basis, suggesting that participation on an as-converted basis would be economically favorable for all preferred holders." **This is incorrect as to Series C.** The as-converted value (~$13.95 simple; ~$12.59 in equilibrium) is **below** the Series C $18.50 preference, so Series C is better off taking its preference (break-even) than converting. The minutes also state the implied per-share equity value "exceeded the current 409A valuation" — but the implied value ($13.95/$12.59) is **below** the 409A fair market value of $14.30 used for the Malhotra and Yeung grants. These errors should be corrected, and the Board (and Special Committee) should be advised of the accurate waterfall before acting on definitive documentation. The errors are relevant to the fiduciary-duty analysis (Area 9) because they suggest the Board may have been materially misinformed about Series C's economics.

## VII. Area 4 — Transfer Restrictions

### A. ROFR and Co-Sale Do Not Apply to the Merger

The Stockholders' Agreement imposes a general transfer restriction (§2.1), a right of first refusal (§2.2), a co-sale right (§2.3), and tag-along rights (§2.4) on Transfers of Shares by Restricted Stockholders. The merger does **not** trigger these provisions, for two independent reasons:

1. **The merger is not a "Transfer" by a stockholder.** A "Transfer" (§1.1) is a sale, assignment, or disposition of Shares *by a stockholder* to a transferee. In the merger, the Company's shares are **cancelled and converted** into the right to receive consideration by operation of the merger statute; no stockholder "transfers" shares to Hargrove. ROFR/co-sale are designed for voluntary secondary transfers, not change-of-control mergers.

2. **Sales of the Company are expressly carved out.** §2.1(c) permits Transfers "in connection with a Sale of the Company consummated in accordance with Article 3," and §2.7(e) lists the same as a "Permitted Transfer." Co-sale expressly does not apply to Permitted Transfers (§2.3(d)). The merger is a Sale of the Company (Area 1).

### B. The "Consummated in Accordance with Article 3" Qualifier — Minor Ambiguity

Both §2.1(c) and §2.7(e) condition the carve-out on the Sale of the Company being "consummated in accordance with Article 3" (the drag-along article). Two readings:

- **Better reading:** "in accordance with Article 3" requires compliance with Article 3's *terms* (§3.2 — same per-share consideration on an as-converted basis, waterfall distribution, pro rata cost-sharing, limited reps, capped several indemnity), not that the drag-along must be *affirmatively triggered*. A Sale of the Company in which all stockholders voluntarily consent still complies with Article 3's terms.
- **Alternative reading:** the drag-along must be triggered (Drag-Along Approval obtained) for the carve-out to apply. Under this reading, because the drag-along cannot reliably be triggered without Stonemill (Area 2), the transfer-restriction carve-out would be unavailable without Stonemill — a circular and odd result.

We recommend the better reading, but the ambiguity should be **clarified in the merger agreement** (e.g., by obtaining a waiver/consent from the parties or a confirmatory interpretation) to eliminate any argument that ROFR/co-sale apply.

### C. Tag-Along Does Not Apply

Tag-along (§2.4) applies only to a "Founder Transfer" — a Founder's transfer of >5% of outstanding Shares to a third party. The merger is a Sale of the Company, not a Founder Transfer, and results in all stockholders receiving consideration. Tag-along is inapplicable.

### D. Termination of the Stockholders' Agreement

The Stockholders' Agreement **terminates upon a Sale of the Company** (§6.1(b)). Upon termination, all transfer restrictions, ROFR, co-sale, tag-along, and drag-along rights "cease to be of any further force and effect," and the Company must remove related legends (§6.3). Because the merger is a Sale of the Company, the Stockholders' Agreement terminates at closing. ✓

**Net result (Area 4):** The merger does not trigger ROFR, co-sale, or tag-along; the Stockholders' Agreement terminates at closing. The only open item is the minor "Article 3" qualifier, which should be clarified in the merger agreement. **Severity: Moderate.**

With a target closing in Q1 2025 (before November 3, 2026), the Sale-of-the-Company termination governs, and the sunset is moot. **No lingering governance rights** — provided the merger closes. If the merger fails to close, the Voting Agreement (and the observer right) continue until November 3, 2026.

### D. Board Observer Right (§4)

Stonemill's non-voting observer right (entitled to attend all Board meetings and receive all Board materials) terminates upon closing for the same reasons (Stonemill ceases to hold ≥1.0M Series C; the Voting Agreement terminates). **The observer right does not survive the merger.** ✓

Two process points during the pre-closing period:
- The observer was **absent from the April 17, 2024 meeting** (where the Board authorized Hargrove engagement). We must confirm the observer was properly notified and received materials for that meeting; if not, Stonemill may claim a breach of its contractual information rights, reinforcing its objection. (§4.2 permits exclusion only to preserve privilege or address a bona fide conflict — the minutes do not indicate any such exclusion.)
- During the period between signing and closing, the observer right remains in effect, and Stonemill is entitled to Board materials relating to the Transaction. The Company should expect Stonemill to exercise this right aggressively.

**Net result (Area 5):** The Voting Agreement and all designation/observer rights terminate at closing; no lingering rights if the merger closes before November 3, 2026. **Severity: Moderate** (clean termination; main risk is the pre-closing observer/information dynamic and the failure-to-close scenario).

## IX. Area 6 — Cross-Document Definitional Inconsistencies

### A. "Liquidation Event" vs. "Sale of the Company" vs. "Deemed Liquidation Event"

| Term | Document | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **Liquidation Event** | Restated Cert. §4.3.2(d) | (i) merger/combination where stockholders retain <50% voting power of survivor; (ii) sale/lease/transfer/exclusive license of all or substantially all assets; (iii) transaction transferring >50% voting power **(other than an equity financing primarily for capital-raising)**; (iv) voluntary/involuntary liquidation/dissolution/winding up. |
| **Sale of the Company** | Stockholders' Ag. §1.1; Voting Ag. §1 | (i) merger/consolidation/reorganization where stockholders retain <majority voting power of survivor; (ii) sale/lease/exclusive license/disposition of all or substantially all assets; (iii) transaction transferring >50% voting power. **No capital-raising exception; no liquidation/dissolution prong.** |
| **Deemed Liquidation Event** | RRA §9(c) | **Undefined.** |

**Inconsistencies:**

1. **Capital-raising exception.** The Restated Certificate's "Liquidation Event" excludes equity financements primarily for capital-raising (§4.3.2(d)(iii)); the Stockholders'/Voting Agreement "Sale of the Company" contains **no such exception.** A capital-raising transaction that transfers >50% voting power (e.g., a heavily dilutive down round) would be a "Sale of the Company" (triggering drag-along/termination) but **not** a "Liquidation Event" (no preference/waterfall). This divergence does not affect the all-cash Hargrove merger (which is captured by both), but it is a latent inconsistency that could matter in other scenarios.

2. **Liquidation/dissolution.** The Restated Certificate's "Liquidation Event" includes voluntary/involuntary liquidation, dissolution, or winding up (§4.3.2(d)(iv)); the "Sale of the Company" definitions do **not.** A dissolution would trigger liquidation preferences but would not be a "Sale of the Company" (no drag-along, no Stockholders'/Voting Agreement termination). Again, not relevant to the merger, but a latent gap.

3. **"Deemed Liquidation Event" is undefined in the RRA.** This is the most consequential inconsistency for the Transaction. The RRA terminates registration rights on a "Deemed Liquidation Event" (§9(c)) but never defines the term. The Restated Certificate uses "Liquidation Event" (not "Deemed Liquidation Event"). The "Deemed" prefix signals an intent to capture *deemed* liquidations (i.e., change-of-control transactions treated as liquidations), which aligns with the Restated Certificate's "Liquidation Event" — but the term is not cross-referenced or defined. **This creates ambiguity about whether the RRA terminates on the merger.** See Area 10.

### B. "IPO" Definitions Vary

| Document | IPO Definition |
|---|---|
| Restated Cert. §4.3.3(b)(i) | Firm-commitment underwritten IPO; **≥$75M gross proceeds AND ≥$55.50/share** (3x Series C OIP) |
| Stockholders' Ag. §6.1(a) | Firm-commitment underwritten public offering; **≥$75M gross proceeds** (no price threshold) |
| Voting Ag. §7.1(a) | Firm-commitment underwritten IPO; **≥$75M gross proceeds** (no price threshold) |
| RRA §1 | "First underwritten public offering of Common Stock under the Securities Act" (**no proceeds threshold**) |

These divergences affect when mandatory conversion (charter) and agreement terminations fire, but they are **not directly relevant to the all-cash merger** (which is not an IPO). Noted for completeness as a Moderate inconsistency.

### C. Recommendation

The merger agreement should (i) include a clear statement that the Transaction constitutes a Liquidation Event / Sale of the Company / Deemed Liquidation Event for all purposes, and (ii) include express terminations/waivers of the RRA and the other governance agreements at closing to eliminate any definitional ambiguity. **Severity: Moderate** (with the "Deemed Liquidation Event" gap elevated to Significant in the Area 10 context).

### A. Document Gap — Critical

We have **not received** the 2021 Equity Incentive Plan (the "Plan") or any individual award agreements (option agreements, RSU agreements). Without these, we **cannot determine**:
- (a) whether the merger constitutes a "change of control" under the Plan;
- (b) whether unvested awards accelerate on a **single-trigger** or **double-trigger** basis (or are cancelled/cashed out/rolled over);
- (c) the aggregate dollar impact of any acceleration on deal economics; and
- (d) whether the Plan grants option holders voting or consent rights in a merger.

**This is a Critical document gap.** The Plan and all individual award agreements must be requested immediately (see document request list, Section XV).

| Award | Holder | Grant Date | Shares | Exercise/Strike | Vesting |
|---|---|---|---:|---:|---|
| Stock options | Raj Malhotra (VP Engineering) | Apr. 12, 2023 | 350,000 | $14.30 | 4-yr, 1-yr cliff, monthly thereafter |
| RSUs | Linda Yeung (CFO) | Apr. 17, 2024 | 200,000 | n/a | 3-yr, 1/3 per year |

### C. Preliminary Economic Observations (Subject to Plan Terms)

- **Malhotra options (350,000 @ $14.30).** The implied common per-share value in the waterfall is ~$12.59 (and the simple as-converted value is ~$13.95). Both are **below** the $14.30 exercise price, so these options are **underwater** at the Transaction price. If the consideration per common share is ~$12.59, the options have **no intrinsic value** and would likely be cancelled for no consideration (or cashed out at $0) at closing, unless the Plan provides otherwise. Acceleration would add **~$0** to deal economics for these options. (Confirm the exact per-common-share consideration and Plan treatment.)
- **Yeung RSUs (200,000).** RSUs have no exercise price. As of the target closing (Q1 2025, ~10 months after the April 17, 2024 grant), **no tranche will have vested** (first tranche vests April 17, 2025). If the Plan provides **single-trigger** acceleration on a change of control, all 200,000 RSUs would accelerate and cash out at ~$12.59/share ≈ **~$2.5M**. If **double-trigger** (change of control + termination), acceleration would occur only if Yeung is terminated in connection with the closing — likely $0 if she is retained. The treatment of unvested RSUs in the merger (cash-out vs. cancellation vs. rollover) is governed by the Plan and merger agreement.
- **Aggregate option pool.** The "Various Common Holders / Option Pool" line (10,100,000 shares) includes both actual common shares and options/RSUs. Based on the July 2023 minutes (~7.42M shares subject to outstanding awards or exercised under the then-8.0M reserve, plus the 200,000 Yeung RSU grant), outstanding options/RSUs are roughly **~7.6–8.0M shares.** The aggregate acceleration impact cannot be quantified without the Plan, award agreements, and a current cap table with vesting detail. **This must be quantified for deal economics.**

### D. Equity Plan Share Reserve Increase (July 19, 2023) — Stockholder Approval Status Unknown

At the July 19, 2023 meeting, the Board approved increasing the Plan share reserve from 8,000,000 to 9,500,000 shares (a 1,500,000-share increase). The minutes reflect **Board approval only (7-0)**; outside counsel Rourke "noted that the amendment required Board approval" but **did not address stockholder approval.**

- **Charter protective provisions:** The increase is a *Plan* amendment, not an increase in *authorized* capital stock (authorized Common remains 50,000,000). Accordingly, Restated Cert. §4.3.6(a)(ii) (consent to change authorized shares) likely does **not** apply. The anti-dilution exception (§4.3.4(b)(i)) exempts shares issued under Board-approved equity plans (including at least one Preferred-designated director), so Plan issuances do not trigger anti-dilution adjustments. No Preferred consent appears required for the reserve increase itself.
- **Stockholder approval:** Whether stockholder approval was *required* (under the Plan terms, DGCL, or applicable tax/409A rules) and, if required, *obtained*, is **unknown.** Many equity plans require stockholder approval for share-reserve increases. If stockholder approval was required and not obtained, the increase — and grants made from the increased reserve (including potentially the Yeung RSUs) — could be vulnerable to challenge. **We must obtain the Plan and any stockholder consent records.** **Severity: Significant.**

### E. Recommendations

1. Request the 2021 Equity Incentive Plan (as amended), all individual award agreements, the current cap table with vesting schedules, and any stockholder consent records relating to the Plan or the July 2023 amendment.
2. Quantify the aggregate acceleration/cash-out exposure and reflect it in the deal model and the merger agreement's treatment-of-awards provisions.
3. Confirm whether the merger agreement will provide for cash-out, cancellation, or rollover of unvested awards, and obtain the necessary Plan and award-level consents.
4. Confirm the per-common-share merger consideration to assess whether options are in or out of the money.

## XI. Area 8 — Authorization of Corporate Actions: Board Minutes Review

We reviewed all eight sets of Board minutes (January 18, 2023 through September 25, 2024). Most actions were properly authorized by a quorum of the Board. Three items require flagging.

### A. Clearfield National Bank Term Loan ($12M, October 11, 2023) — CRITICAL

At the October 11, 2023 meeting, the Board approved a $12M secured term loan with Clearfield National Bank (5-year term, SOFR + 3.50%, secured by substantially all assets including accounts receivable, inventory, equipment, and IP). The resolution was approved 7-0.

**Problem:** Restated Cert. §4.3.6(a)(v) prohibits the Company from incurring "indebtedness for borrowed money in excess of $5,000,000 in the aggregate outstanding at any time" without the prior written consent or affirmative vote of holders of **more than 50% of the outstanding Preferred Stock** (voting as a single class on an as-converted basis; threshold >8,250,000 shares). The $12M term loan **exceeds $5M** and does **not** fit either exception: (A) equipment financing/capital leases ≤ $2M (the loan is a general secured term loan, not equipment financing, and far exceeds $2M); (B) trade payables in the ordinary course (inapplicable).

**The minutes reflect Board approval but do not reflect any Preferred Stockholder consent.** Board approval (even unanimous, including the Preferred-designated directors voting as directors) is **not** the same as the stockholder-level consent required by §4.3.6(a)(v). The protective provision requires a separate written consent or affirmative vote of the Preferred holders as stockholders.

**Implications:**
- If the Required Preferred consent was **not** obtained, the Company may have breached the charter protective provision in entering the term loan. Preferred holders (including Stonemill) may have a claim and could seek to challenge the loan or assert damages.
- This is **additional leverage for Stonemill:** a potential breach claim that Stonemill could waive in exchange for Transaction concessions.
- The loan is to be repaid at closing (reflected in the $473M equity value), so the consent issue does not directly block the Transaction, but it is a governance failure that must be disclosed and addressed.
- Confirm whether the loan agreement contains a change-of-control covenant that could trigger a default pre-closing (request the loan agreement).

**Action:** Request all Preferred Stockholder written-consent records relating to the Clearfield term loan. If no consent was obtained, assess remediation (e.g., a ratifying consent) and disclosure obligations. **Severity: Critical.**

### B. Equity Plan Share Reserve Increase (July 19, 2023) — Significant

As discussed in Area 7, the Board approved the 8.0M → 9.5M reserve increase (7-0), but the minutes do not address stockholder approval. **Severity: Significant.** Action: request the Plan and stockholder consent records.

### C. SensorLink Technologies LOI — CRITICAL / TIME-SENSITIVE

At the January 24, 2024 meeting, the Board authorized the CEO to negotiate and execute a non-binding LOI with SensorLink Technologies, Inc. for a potential acquisition (up to $22M). The LOI was executed **March 8, 2024.** At the September 25, 2024 meeting, the CEO reported that the LOI contained a **mutual exclusivity provision** with an initial six-month term expiring **September 8, 2024**, and an **automatic renewal** clause: the exclusivity period "automatically extended for successive ninety (90)-day periods unless either party provided written notice of termination to the other party at least fifteen (15) days prior to the expiration of the then-current exclusivity period."

**Critical facts:**
- To prevent the first auto-renewal, written termination notice had to be delivered **on or before August 24, 2024** (15 days before September 8, 2024).
- The CEO acknowledged at the September 25 meeting that he was **"not certain whether a formal written termination notice had been delivered."** Outside counsel Rourke flagged the auto-renewal and recommended immediate confirmation/termination.
- **No termination notice appears to have been sent before September 8, 2024.** Accordingly, the exclusivity **auto-renewed on September 8, 2024** for a 90-day period running through approximately **December 7, 2024.** To prevent the *next* renewal, notice must be delivered on or before approximately **November 22, 2024.**

**Implications — potential deal conflict:**
- Terravox may be subject to a **live exclusivity obligation to SensorLink** that runs through at least December 7, 2024 (and continues to auto-renew unless terminated). If the LOI's exclusivity prohibits Terravox from negotiating or signing a definitive agreement with another party (Hargrove) during the exclusivity period, **Terravox's signing of a definitive merger agreement with Hargrove could breach the SensorLink LOI**, exposing Terravox to contractual liability and giving SensorLink a potential claim (and possibly a basis to seek to enjoin or delay the Hargrove transaction).
- This is a **time-sensitive issue that could directly conflict with the ability to sign a definitive agreement with Hargrove.** Per the engagement email, this should be flagged immediately.

**Action (urgent):**
1. Immediately confirm whether a written termination notice was sent to SensorLink after the September 25 meeting, and the date and form of any such notice.
2. If no effective notice was sent, **send written termination notice immediately** (to be effective for the December 7, 2024 renewal, it must be delivered on or before ~November 22, 2024; earlier is better).
3. **Obtain a mutual termination/waiver agreement from SensorLink** — this is the cleanest and fastest path to extinguish the exclusivity and any associated obligations.
4. **Request and review the full SensorLink LOI** to identify (a) the exact scope of the exclusivity (what activities are prohibited), (b) any standstill, non-solicitation, expense-reimbursement, or break-up fee provisions, (c) the governing law and dispute-resolution mechanism, and (d) the survival of any obligations post-termination.
5. Assess whether any breach has already occurred (e.g., if Terravox engaged with Hargrove during the exclusivity period) and the resulting exposure.
6. Address the SensorLink exclusivity in the merger agreement (e.g., as a disclosure schedule item and a closing condition / bring-down).

**Severity: Critical / time-sensitive.** This is the item most likely to be a deal-breaker or to delay signing, and it should be escalated to Jonathan Hsieh immediately, ahead of the full memo.

## XII. Area 9 — Fiduciary Duty and Sale-Process Concerns

### A. Special Committee Composition — Significant Vulnerability

The Special Committee, formed at the April 12, 2023 meeting, consists of **Rebecca Lau (Chair), Thomas Garrett, and Sandra Okafor.** The minutes expressly note that Okafor "is a director designated by Northbridge Growth Equity Fund IV, LP pursuant to [the Voting Agreement]."

**Concern.** Under Delaware law (Revlon, CNX Gas, et al.), a special committee evaluating a sale-of-control transaction should be composed of **disinterested and independent** directors to invoke the business judgment rule and withstand fiduciary-duty challenges. Okafor is **neither disinterested nor independent**: she is a Principal at Northbridge and Northbridge's Board designee, and Northbridge holds 5,200,000 shares of Series B Preferred with a $48.1M liquidation preference and a distinct economic position in the waterfall (Series B converts and receives upside). Her duty as a director runs to all stockholders, but her structural role as a specific stockholder's designee creates a conflict and undermines the committee's independence.

**Why it matters.** A compromised Special Committee weakens the defense against a fiduciary-duty challenge — precisely the kind of challenge Stonemill (via Trask) is positioned to bring, and has signaled it may bring. The conflict is exacerbated by the Voting Agreement's committee-composition requirement (§2.4): each committee must include at least one Investor Stockholder designee unless the Investor Stockholders agree otherwise in writing. This creates a **tension between the Voting Agreement's mandate and fiduciary best practices.**

**Recommendations:**
1. **Reconstitute the Special Committee** to exclude Okafor, leaving Lau and Garrett (both independent), and obtain the **written agreement of the Investor Stockholders** to waive the §2.4 Investor-designee requirement (or substitute a truly independent director). If a third member is needed, add a new independent director rather than another Investor designee.
2. **Engage independent legal counsel for the Special Committee**, separate from Avery, Pollard & Glenn (which is Company counsel). The minutes show Rourke (Company counsel) advising the committee — this is a process weakness. Independent committee counsel is a standard Revlon protection.
3. Ensure the **Thornbury & Associates fairness opinion** is finalized, delivered, and considered by the (reconstituted) committee before any approval of definitive documentation.
4. Document the sale process thoroughly (market check, alternatives considered, negotiation history) to support Revlon compliance.
5. Consider whether Okafor's participation to date taints prior committee actions and whether ratification or re-approval by a clean committee is advisable.

**Severity: Significant.**

### B. Trask's Absence and Objection — Process Vulnerability

At the April 17, 2024 meeting (where the Board authorized engagement with Hargrove), Trask was absent (as was Garrett). The Stonemill observer was also absent. The resolution passed 5-0 and is **valid Board action** (quorum of 4 satisfied; 5 present). Trask's absence does not invalidate the action.

However, Trask objected by email on **April 19, 2024**, stating that the Board acted "without the full Board's consideration and without Stonemill's input," that Stonemill "has significant economic and governance rights in connection with any potential change-of-control transaction," and that "no further substantive steps be taken ... without Stonemill's participation." This objection is appended to the April 17 minutes.

Trask subsequently **voted against** the Hargrove exclusivity (July 10, 2024) and the definitive-doc authorization (September 25, 2024), and **abstained** on the approval of the April 17 and July 10 minutes. This establishes a **consistent record of dissent** that Stonemill can use to argue the sale process was procedurally deficient — that the Board proceeded without full participation and without consulting a major stockholder holding governance rights. While not legally invalidating, this record is **exploitable ammunition** for Stonemill in any challenge or negotiation.

**Recommendations:** Ensure all future Board actions on the Transaction are taken with full participation where possible; document Trask's opportunities to participate and any refusals; ensure the Stonemill observer receives all materials going forward; and preserve a clean record of the process. **Severity: Significant.**

### C. Limited Auction / Exclusivity — Revlon Exposure

The sale process involved periods of exclusivity (first with "Beta," terminated October 2023; then with Hargrove, 60 days from July 2024) without a broad public auction. Trask objected to the Beta exclusivity (July 2023) as "insufficiently broad." A limited-auction process is **defensible** — the Board engaged Meridian, contacted multiple parties, received multiple indications of interest, and the Hargrove interest was inbound — but it is a **Revlon vulnerability** if the price or process is challenged. The reconstituted independent committee, independent counsel, and Thornbury fairness opinion are the principal mitigants. **Severity: Moderate.**

### D. Indemnification and D&O Insurance

The Restated Certificate (Art. VII) and Bylaws (Art. VI) provide broad D&O indemnification and advancement, and the Company has entered individual indemnification agreements with each director (Bylaws §6.4) and maintains D&O insurance of at least $5M per occurrence (Stockholders' Ag. §5.3). Post-closing, former directors (including Trask, Lau, Garrett, Okafor, Kleinhans, Ellery, Chandrasekaran) may face claims arising from the Transaction. The merger agreement should provide for a **tail (run-off) D&O insurance policy** (typically six years) for former directors and officers, and should address the survival of indemnification obligations. **Severity: Moderate.**

### E. Exclusive Forum

The Bylaws (Art. VIII) select the Delaware Court of Chancery as the exclusive forum for derivative and fiduciary-duty claims and internal-affairs claims, and federal courts for Securities Act claims. Any Stonemill challenge to the Transaction or the sale process would be brought in Delaware Chancery, which is favorable for predictability. Note: the federal-forum provision for Securities Act claims was held unenforceable in *Cohen v. Brown-Forman* (Del. Ch. 2021) as to non-consenting stockholders, but this does not affect the Delaware-forum provision for fiduciary/internal-affairs claims, which is the relevant provision for a Stonemill challenge.

## XIII. Area 10 — Registration Rights Post-Transaction

### A. RRA Termination Triggers (§9)

Registration rights terminate with respect to a particular Holder upon the earliest of: (a) the Holder can sell all Registrable Securities in a single three-month period under Rule 144 without volume/manner-of-sale restrictions and without the current-public-information requirement; (b) the fifth anniversary of the closing of the Company's IPO; or (c) **a "Deemed Liquidation Event."**

### B. The "Deemed Liquidation Event" Problem

The RRA uses the term "Deemed Liquidation Event" in §9(c) but **does not define it** (Area 6). The Restated Certificate uses "Liquidation Event" (not "Deemed Liquidation Event"). The intent is presumably that registration rights terminate on a change-of-control transaction (the "Deemed" prefix signals deemed-liquidation treatment), which would align with the Restated Certificate's "Liquidation Event" and capture the merger. But because the term is undefined, there is **ambiguity** about whether the RRA terminates on the merger:
- If "Deemed Liquidation Event" = the Restated Certificate's "Liquidation Event" (which captures the merger), the RRA terminates on the merger. ✓
- If "Deemed Liquidation Event" is read narrowly (e.g., only an actual liquidation/dissolution), the RRA would **not** terminate on the merger, creating post-closing registration-rights liability.

### C. Practical Effect in an All-Cash Deal

In the Transaction, all Terravox equity is cancelled and converted into the right to receive **cash**. "Registrable Securities" are defined (RRA §1) as shares of Common Stock issued or issuable upon conversion of the Preferred held by the Holders (and related dividend/exchange shares). After the merger, the Preferred is cancelled and no Registrable Securities remain. Accordingly, **even if the RRA does not formally terminate, there are no Registrable Securities to register post-closing**, rendering the registration rights effectively moot in an all-cash deal. (The analysis would differ if the consideration included Hargrove equity, but it does not.)

### D. Recommendation

To eliminate ambiguity and avoid any post-closing argument, the **merger agreement should include an express termination and waiver of the RRA at closing**, with the Holders' consents obtained (the RRA can be terminated by written consent of the Company and Holders of at least a majority of the then-outstanding Registrable Securities — §9). The drag-along, if validly triggered, could be argued to compel Holder consent to RRA termination as a matter "required to be approved in connection with" the Sale (§3.1(a)(A)), but reliance on the drag-along is uncertain (Area 2). The cleaner approach is to obtain the Holder consents directly at closing. **Severity: Significant** (ambiguity) / **Moderate** (practical effect moot in all-cash deal).

## Appendix B — Waterfall Analysis

**Assumptions:** Equity value $473M ($485M EV − $12M debt). Preferred is non-participating (Restated Cert. §4.3.2(b)); each holder elects between its liquidation preference and conversion (deemed election for the greater return). Fully diluted shares ~33.9M (per Stockholders' Ag. Exhibit B). Simple as-converted value = $473M ÷ 33.9M ≈ **$13.95/share.**

**Step 1 — Test conversion for all series:**

| Series | Preference/share | Convert/share ($13.95) | Election |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| Series C (Stonemill) | $18.50 | $13.95 | **Preference** (>$13.95) |
| Series B (Northbridge) | $9.25 | $13.95 | **Convert** (>$9.25) |
| Series A (Ridgeline) | $4.00 | $13.95 | **Convert** (>$4.00) |

**Step 2 — Equilibrium (Series C takes preference; A, B, and Common convert):**

- Series C preference paid: 7,800,000 × $18.50 = **$144,300,000**
- Remaining for converting shares: $473,000,000 − $144,300,000 = **$328,700,000**
- Converting shares: 33,900,000 − 7,800,000 = **26,100,000**
- Per converting share: $328,700,000 ÷ 26,100,000 ≈ **$12.59/share**

**Step 3 — Verify equilibrium (re-test):**

| Series | Preference/share | Convert/share ($12.59) | Election | Consistent? |
|---|---:|---:|---|---|
| Series C | $18.50 | $12.59 | Preference | ✓ |
| Series B | $9.25 | $12.59 | Convert | ✓ |
| Series A | $4.00 | $12.59 | Convert | ✓ |

**Step 4 — Distribution:**

| Holder | Shares | $/Share | Total | vs. Preference | Upside/(Loss) |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Stonemill (Series C) | 7,800,000 | $18.50 (pref.) | $144,300,000 | $144,300,000 | **$0 (break-even)** |
| Northbridge (Series B) | 5,200,000 | $12.59 (convert) | $65,468,000 | $48,100,000 | +$17,368,000 |
| Ridgeline (Series A) | 3,500,000 | $12.59 (convert) | $44,065,000 | $14,000,000 | +$30,065,000 |
| Common (incl. option pool) | 17,400,000 | $12.59 (convert) | $219,066,000 | n/a | n/a |
| **Total** | **33,900,000** | | **~$472,900,000** | | |

*(Totals approximate due to rounding; figures reconcile to ~$473M.)*

**Key conclusions:**

1. **Stonemill (Series C) receives exactly its $18.50 Original Issue Price — break-even on its $144.3M investment — and is the only major stockholder with no upside.** This is the economic root of Stonemill's leverage and its interest in extracting concessions.
2. Series B and Series A convert and receive meaningful upside over their preferences.
3. The common per-share value (~$12.59) is **below** the 409A fair market value of $14.30 used for recent grants, meaning options struck at or above ~$12.59 (including the Malhotra options at $14.30) are **underwater** at the Transaction price.
4. The September 25, 2024 Board minutes' statement that "all holders of Preferred Stock would receive amounts in excess of their respective liquidation preferences on an as-converted basis" is **incorrect as to Series C** (Series C receives its preference, not an excess, and would not convert). The minutes' statement that the implied per-share value "exceeded the current 409A valuation" is also inconsistent with the math ($13.95/$12.59 < $14.30). These errors should be corrected and the Board/Special Committee properly advised before definitive-doc approval.

*This analysis is preliminary and based on the figures in the Board minutes and the Stockholders' Agreement capitalization schedule. It should be confirmed against the Company's current cap table and the final merger consideration allocation, and reconciled with Thornbury & Associates' valuation work.*

---

## Appendix C — Document Inventory Reviewed

1. Third Amended and Restated Certificate of Incorporation of Terravox Systems, Inc., dated November 3, 2021 (the "Restated Certificate").
2. Amended and Restated Bylaws of Terravox Systems, Inc., last amended November 3, 2021 (the "Bylaws").
3. Stockholders' Agreement, dated November 3, 2021, among the Company, the Founders, and the Investor Stockholders.
4. Voting Agreement, dated November 3, 2021, among the Company, the Founders, and the Investor Stockholders.
5. Registration Rights Agreement, dated November 3, 2021, among the Company and the Investor Stockholders (the "RRA").
6. Board minutes, January 18, 2023 through September 25, 2024 (eight meetings).
7. Engagement email from Jonathan Hsieh, dated October 14, 2024 (scope and priority areas).

**Documents identified as missing and requested:** 2021 Equity Incentive Plan (as amended) and individual award agreements; Preferred Stockholder consent records for the Clearfield term loan; the Clearfield loan agreement and security agreements; the full SensorLink LOI; stockholder consent records for the July 2023 plan amendment; Board observer notice records for April 17, 2024; Special Committee charter and advisor engagement letters; Thornbury fairness opinion; indemnification agreements; D&O insurance policy; current cap table.

---

*Prepared by [Associate], Whitfield & Crane LLP. This memorandum is privileged and confidential attorney work product. Please direct questions to the undersigned; route all document requests and external communications through Jonathan Hsieh.*"}

from docx import Document
from docx.shared import Pt, Inches, RGBColor
from docx.enum.text import WD_LINE_SPACING

doc = Document()

# --- Narrower margins ---
for s in doc.sections:
    s.top_margin = Inches(0.8)
    s.bottom_margin = Inches(0.8)
    s.left_margin = Inches(0.9)
    s.right_margin = Inches(0.9)

styles = doc.styles

def set_font(style, size, bold=False, color=None, name='Calibri'):
    style.font.name = name
    style.font.size = Pt(size)
    style.font.bold = bold
    if color:
        style.font.color.rgb = color

def set_para(style, before=0, after=6, line=1.0, keep_with_next=False):
    pf = style.paragraph_format
    pf.space_before = Pt(before)
    pf.space_after = Pt(after)
    pf.line_spacing = line
    pf.line_spacing_rule = WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE if line == 1.0 else WD_LINE_SPACING.MULTIPLE
    pf.keep_with_next = keep_with_next

# Normal / body
normal = styles['Normal']
set_font(normal, 10.5)
set_para(normal, before=0, after=6, line=1.05)

# First Paragraph (pandoc uses this for first para after heading) -> same as normal
try:
    fp = styles['First Paragraph']
    set_font(fp, 10.5)
    set_para(fp, after=6, line=1.05)
except KeyError:
    pass

# Body Text
try:
    bt = styles['Body Text']
    set_font(bt, 10.5)
    set_para(bt, after=6, line=1.05)
except KeyError:
    pass

# Compact (list items)
try:
    cp = styles['Compact']
    set_font(cp, 10.5)
    set_para(cp, after=2, line=1.0)
except KeyError:
    pass

# Block Text (blockquotes)
try:
    bl = styles['Block Text']
    set_font(bl, 10)
    set_para(bl, after=6, line=1.05)
except KeyError:
    pass

# Headings
h1 = styles['Heading 1']
set_font(h1, 14, bold=True, color=RGBColor(0x1F,0x38,0x64))
set_para(h1, before=14, after=4, keep_with_next=True)

h2 = styles['Heading 2']
set_font(h2, 12.5, bold=True, color=RGBColor(0x1F,0x38,0x64))
set_para(h2, before=11, after=3, keep_with_next=True)

h3 = styles['Heading 3']
set_font(h3, 11, bold=True, color=RGBColor(0x2E,0x4B,0x7A))
set_para(h3, before=8, after=2, keep_with_next=True)

h4 = styles['Heading 4']
set_font(h4, 10.5, bold=True, color=RGBColor(0x2E,0x4B,0x7A))
set_para(h4, before=6, after=2, keep_with_next=True)

# Title
try:
    ti = styles['Title']
    set_font(ti, 16, bold=True, color=RGBColor(0x1F,0x38,0x64))
    set_para(ti, before=0, after=6)
except KeyError:
    pass

# Table styles - make table text smaller
try:
    for tname in ['Table','Table Grid','Light List Accent 1','Compact']:
        pass
except Exception:
    pass

doc.save('output/reference-compact.docx')
print('wrote output/reference-compact.docx')
PYEOF
python3 make_ref.py 2>&1"}

text: The tables inherit 10.5pt with 6pt after-spacing per cell paragraph plus default cell padding — that's the space driver. Let me post-process the generated docx to compress table fonts, cell spacing, and cell margins.

tool_calls.name: bash
tool_calls.arguments:
{"command": "cd $WORKSPACE_DIR && cat > compress.py << 'PYEOF'
from docx import Document
from docx.shared import Pt, Twips
from docx.enum.text import WD_LINE_SPACING
from docx.oxml.ns import qn
from docx.oxml import OxmlElement

doc = Document('output/governance-diligence-memo.docx')

# 1) Tighten table cell paragraphs: no before/after, single line, 9pt font
for tbl in doc.tables:
    # set cell margins via tblPr
    tblPr = tbl._tbl.tblPr
    # remove existing tblCellMar if any
    for existing in tblPr.findall(qn('w:tblCellMar')):
        tblPr.remove(existing)
    mar = OxmlElement('w:tblCellMar')
    for side, val in (('top','20'),('bottom','20'),('start','60'),('end','60'),('left','60'),('right','60')):
        e = OxmlElement('w:'+side)
        e.set(qn('w:w'), val)
        e.set(qn('w:type'),'dxa')
        mar.append(e)
    tblPr.append(mar)
    for row in tbl.rows:
        for cell in row.cells:
            for p in cell.paragraphs:
                pf = p.paragraph_format
                pf.space_before = Pt(0)
                pf.space_after = Pt(0)
                pf.line_spacing = 1.0
                pf.line_spacing_rule = WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE
                for r in p.runs:
                    r.font.size = Pt(9)
                    r.font.name = 'Calibri'

# 2) Tighten body Normal and Compact spacing a bit more
def tighten(style_name, after, before=0, line=1.03):
    try:
        st = doc.styles[style_name]
        pf = st.paragraph_format
        pf.space_after = Pt(after)
        pf.space_before = Pt(before)
        pf.line_spacing = line
        pf.line_spacing_rule = WD_LINE_SPACING.MULTIPLE if line != 1.0 else WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE
    except KeyError:
        pass

tighten('Normal', 5, 0, 1.05)
tighten('First Paragraph', 5, 0, 1.05)
tighten('Body Text', 5, 0, 1.05)
tighten('Compact', 1, 0, 1.0)
tighten('Block Text', 4, 0, 1.03)

# 3) Slightly reduce heading spacing
def head(style_name, before, after):
    try:
        st = doc.styles[style_name]
        pf = st.paragraph_format
        pf.space_before = Pt(before)
        pf.space_after = Pt(after)
    except KeyError:
        pass
head('Heading 1', 12, 3)
head('Heading 2', 9, 2)
head('Heading 3', 6, 2)
head('Heading 4', 5, 1)

doc.save('output/governance-diligence-memo.docx')
print('compressed and saved')
PYEOF
python3 compress.py 2>&1 && python3 skills/docx/scripts/validate.py output/governance-diligence-memo.docx 2>&1 && rm -f /tmp/governance-diligence-memo.pdf && timeout 120 soffice --headless --convert-to pdf --outdir /tmp output/governance-diligence-memo.docx 2>&1 | tail -1 && pdfinfo /tmp/governance-diligence-memo.pdf 2>/dev/null | grep Pages"}

{"command": "cd $WORKSPACE_DIR && python3 -c "
import subprocess
# Extract text per page
r = subprocess.run(['pdftotext','-layout','/tmp/governance-diligence-memo.pdf','-'], capture_output=True, text=True)
pages = r.stdout.split('\f')
print('Total pages:', len(pages))
for i, pg in enumerate(pages[:40], 1):
    lines = [l for l in pg.splitlines() if l.strip()]
    chars = sum(len(l) for l in lines)
    # flag sparse pages (potential whitespace)
    flag = '  <-- SPARSE' if chars < 1200 and i>1 else ''
    print(f'p{i:2d}: lines={len(lines):3d} chars={chars:5d}{flag}')
" 2>&1"}

text: I found the real problem — the tables are rendering in extremely narrow columns, forcing every cell to wrap vertically (e.g., the heading "C. Liquidation Preferences" stacks as "C. Liquidation / Preferences / (Restated / Cert. §4.3.2)"). This is why the page count is so high. Let me inspect the table width/grid properties.

text: The tables are rendering at only ~5.5" wide (gridCol widths sum to 7918 twips vs. 9647 twips usable) with fixed layout, and the heading after the table is getting squeezed. Let me fix all tables to use the full page width with autofit layout and proportional columns.

tool_calls.name: bash
tool_calls.arguments:
{"command": "cd $WORKSPACE_DIR && cat > fix_tables.py << 'PYEOF'
from docx import Document
from docx.oxml.ns import qn
from docx.oxml import OxmlElement
import copy

doc = Document('output/governance-diligence-memo.docx')
sec = doc.sections[0]
usable = int((sec.page_width.inches - sec.left_margin.inches - sec.right_margin.inches) * 1440)

for tbl in doc.tables:
    tblPr = tbl._tbl.tblPr
    # 1) table width = 100% (pct 5000)
    tw = tblPr.find(qn('w:tblW'))
    if tw is None:
        tw = OxmlElement('w:tblW'); tblPr.append(tw)
    tw.set(qn('w:w'), '5000'); tw.set(qn('w:type'), 'pct')
    # 2) layout = autofit (remove fixed)
    lay = tblPr.find(qn('w:tblLayout'))
    if lay is None:
        lay = OxmlElement('w:tblLayout'); tblPr.append(lay)
    lay.set(qn('w:type'), 'autofit')
    # 3) Redistribute gridCol widths proportionally to fill usable width
    grid = tbl._tbl.find(qn('w:tblGrid'))
    if grid is not None:
        cols = grid.findall(qn('w:gridCol'))
        widths = [int(c.get(qn('w:w')) or 0) for c in cols]
        s = sum(widths)
        if s > 0:
            new = [max(1, int(round(w * usable / s))) for w in widths]
            # adjust last to exact
            new[-1] = usable - sum(new[:-1])
            for c, w in zip(cols, new):
                c.set(qn('w:w'), str(w))
    # 4) Set each cell tcW to pct 5000? No - keep dxa matching grid. Actually set tcW type=pct so cells fill.
    # Simpler: remove tcW overrides so they follow grid.
    for row in tbl.rows:
        for cell in row.cells:
            tcPr = cell._tc.tcPr
            if tcPr is not None:
                tcW = tcPr.find(qn('w:tcW'))
                if tcW is not None:
                    tcPr.remove(tcW)

doc.save('output/governance-diligence-memo.docx')
print('tables fixed to full width autofit; usable twips =', usable)
PYEOF
python3 fix_tables.py 2>&1 && python3 skills/docx/scripts/validate.py output/governance-diligence-memo.docx 2>&1 && rm -f /tmp/governance-diligence-memo.pdf && timeout 120 soffice --headless --convert-to pdf --outdir /tmp output/governance-diligence-memo.docx 2>&1 | tail -1 && pdfinfo /tmp/governance-diligence-memo.pdf 2>/dev/null | grep Pages"}

                c.set(qn('w:w'), str(w))
    # 4) Set each cell tcW to pct 5000? No - keep dxa matching grid. Actually set tcW type=pct so cells fill.
    # Simpler: remove tcW overrides so they follow grid.
    for row in tbl.rows:
        for cell in row.cells:
            tcPr = cell._tc.tcPr
            if tcPr is not None:
                tcW = tcPr.find(qn('w:tcW'))
                if tcW is not None:
                    tcPr.remove(tcW)

doc.save('output/governance-diligence-memo.docx')

text: The bbox coordinates reveal the real issue: all four columns are rendering at x=64.9 (left margin) on separate lines — the table collapsed to a single narrow column. My earlier `tcW` removal broke the fixed layout. Let me regenerate fresh from markdown and apply one clean, comprehensive post-processing script with proper fixed-layout full-width tables.

tool_calls.name: bash
tool_calls.arguments:
{"command": "cd $WORKSPACE_DIR && python3 skills/docx/scripts/generate_from_md.py output/memo-draft.md output/governance-diligence-memo.docx output/reference-compact.docx 2>&1 && cat > finalize.py << 'PYEOF'
from docx import Document
from docx.shared import Pt, Inches
from docx.enum.text import WD_LINE_SPACING
from docx.oxml.ns import qn
from docx.oxml import OxmlElement

doc = Document('output/governance-diligence-memo.docx')
sec = doc.sections[0]
USABLE = int(round((sec.page_width.inches - sec.left_margin.inches - sec.right_margin.inches) * 1440))

# ---------- TABLES: fixed layout, full width, proportional columns, tcW per cell ----------
for tbl in doc.tables:
    tblPr = tbl._tbl.tblPr
    # table width = full usable width in dxa
    tw = tblPr.find(qn('w:tblW'))
    if tw is None:
        tw = OxmlElement('w:tblW'); tblPr.append(tw)
    tw.set(qn('w:w'), str(USABLE)); tw.set(qn('w:type'), 'dxa')
    # fixed layout
    lay = tblPr.find(qn('w:tblLayout'))
    if lay is None:
        lay = OxmlElement('w:tblLayout'); tblPr.append(lay)
    lay.set(qn('w:type'), 'fixed')
    # cell margins (tight)
    for existing in tblPr.findall(qn('w:tblCellMar')):
        tblPr.remove(existing)
    mar = OxmlElement('w:tblCellMar')
    for side, val in (('top','18'),('bottom','18'),('start','54'),('end','54'),('left','54'),('right','54')):
        e = OxmlElement('w:'+side); e.set(qn('w:w'), val); e.set(qn('w:type'),'dxa'); mar.append(e)
    tblPr.append(mar)
    # redistribute gridCol to full width, keep proportions
    grid = tbl._tbl.find(qn('w:tblGrid'))
    cols = grid.findall(qn('w:gridCol')) if grid is not None else []
    widths = [int(c.get(qn('w:w')) or 100) for c in cols]
    s = sum(widths) or 1
    new = [max(1, int(round(w * USABLE / s))) for w in widths]
    if new:
        new[-1] = USABLE - sum(new[:-1])
        for c, w in zip(cols, new):
            c.set(qn('w:w'), str(max(1,w)))
    # set tcW on every cell to match its column width (dxa)
    for row in tbl.rows:
        cells = row.cells
        for idx, cell in enumerate(cells):
            tcPr = cell._tc.tcPr
            if tcPr is None:
                tcPr = OxmlElement('w:tcPr'); cell._tc.insert(0, tcPr)
            tcW = tcPr.find(qn('w:tcW'))
            if tcW is None:
                tcW = OxmlElement('w:tcW'); tcPr.append(tcW)
            cw = new[idx] if idx < len(new) else USABLE//max(1,len(cells))
            tcW.set(qn('w:w'), str(max(1,cw))); tcW.set(qn('w:type'), 'dxa')
            # tight cell paragraphs, 8.5pt
            for p in cell.paragraphs:
                pf = p.paragraph_format
                pf.space_before = Pt(0); pf.space_after = Pt(0)
                pf.line_spacing = 1.0; pf.line_spacing_rule = WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE
                pf.keep_with_next = False; pf.keep_together = False
                for r in p.runs:
                    r.font.size = Pt(8.5); r.font.name = 'Calibri'
    # allow rows to break across pages
    for row in tbl.rows:
        trPr = row._tr.trPr
        if trPr is not None:
            for cs in trPr.findall(qn('w:cantSplit')):
                trPr.remove(cs)

# ---------- BODY paragraphs: direct formatting ----------
for p in doc.paragraphs:
    sn = p.style.name if p.style else ''
    pf = p.paragraph_format
    pf.keep_with_next = False; pf.keep_together = False
    if sn.startswith('Heading'):
        if sn == 'Heading 1':
            pf.space_before=Pt(10); pf.space_after=Pt(3); pf.line_spacing=1.0
            for r in p.runs: r.font.size=Pt(14); r.font.name='Calibri'
        elif sn == 'Heading 2':
            pf.space_before=Pt(8); pf.space_after=Pt(2); pf.line_spacing=1.0
            for r in p.runs: r.font.size=Pt(12.5); r.font.name='Calibri'
        elif sn == 'Heading 3':
            pf.space_before=Pt(6); pf.space_after=Pt(2); pf.line_spacing=1.0
            for r in p.runs: r.font.size=Pt(11); r.font.name='Calibri'
        elif sn == 'Heading 4':
            pf.space_before=Pt(5); pf.space_after=Pt(1); pf.line_spacing=1.0
            for r in p.runs: r.font.size=Pt(10.5); r.font.name='Calibri'
    elif sn == 'Title':
        pf.space_before=Pt(0); pf.space_after=Pt(4); pf.line_spacing=1.0
        for r in p.runs: r.font.size=Pt(15); r.font.name='Calibri'
    else:
        after = 1 if sn == 'Compact' else 4
        pf.space_before=Pt(0); pf.space_after=Pt(after); pf.line_spacing=1.04
        pf.line_spacing_rule = WD_LINE_SPACING.MULTIPLE
        for r in p.runs: r.font.size=Pt(10); r.font.name='Calibri'
        if sn == 'Compact' and pf.left_indent and pf.left_indent.inches > 0.5:
            pf.left_indent = Inches(0.3)

doc.save('output/governance-diligence-memo.docx')
print('finalized; USABLE twips =', USABLE)

text: The section is single-column (not the issue). Let me extract the actual table XML from document.xml to see what LibreOffice is receiving.

tool_calls.name: bash
tool_calls.arguments:
{"command": "cd $WORKSPACE_DIR && python3 -c "
import re
xml = open('/tmp/unpack/word/document.xml').read()
# find first w:tbl ... </w:tbl>
m = re.search(r'<w:tbl>.*?</w:tbl>', xml, re.S)
if m:
    tbl = m.group(0)
    # print just the tblPr and tblGrid and first row's first cell
    import re as r2
    tblPr = r2.search(r'<w:tblPr>.*?</w:tblPr>', tbl, re.S).group(0)
    grid = r2.search(r'<w:tblGrid>.*?</w:tblGrid>', tbl, re.S).group(0)
    print('--- tblPr ---'); print(tblPr[:600])
    print('--- tblGrid ---'); print(grid[:400])
    # first tr, first tc
    tr = r2.search(r'<w:tr[ >].*?</w:tr>', tbl, re.S).group(0)
    tc = r2.search(r'<w:tc>.*?</w:tc>', tr, re.S).group(0)
    print('--- first cell ---'); print(tc[:500])
" 2>&1"}

text: Critical finding: even the *original* pandoc output renders tables as narrow/single-column in LibreOffice — "Ridgeline Ventures Fund II, LP" stacks one word per line. This is a pandoc/LibreOffice table-rendering issue, not caused by my post-processing. Let me check the "Table" style definition, which may be overriding the grid.

tool_calls.name: bash
tool_calls.arguments:
{"command": "cd $WORKSPACE_DIR && python3 -c "
import re
xml = open('/tmp/unpack/word/styles.xml').read()
# find Table style
m = re.search(r'<w:style [^>]*w:styleId=\"Table\"[^>]*>.*?</w:style>', xml, re.S)
if m:
    print(m.group(0)[:1200])
else:
    print('No Table style found')
    # list all styleIds
    ids = re.findall(r'w:styleId=\"([^\"]+)\"', xml)
    print('styleIds:', [i for i in ids if 'able' in i.lower() or 'Table' in i])
" 2>&1"}

text: The tables reference a "Table" style that doesn't exist in styles.xml (only "TableNormal" and "TableGrid" exist) — a dangling reference causing LibreOffice to fall back to narrow default rendering. Let me switch all tables to the existing "TableGrid" style and re-test.

tool_calls.name: bash
tool_calls.arguments:
{"command": "cd $WORKSPACE_DIR && cat > fix_style.py << 'PYEOF'
from docx import Document
from docx.oxml.ns import qn
doc = Document('output/governance-diligence-memo.docx')
for tbl in doc.tables:
    tblPr = tbl._tbl.tblPr
    ts = tblPr.find(qn('w:tblStyle'))
    if ts is not None:
        ts.set(qn('w:val'), 'TableGrid')
    else:
        from docx.oxml import OxmlElement
        ts = OxmlElement('w:tblStyle'); ts.set(qn('w:val'),'TableGrid')
        tblPr.insert(0, ts)
doc.save('output/governance-diligence-memo.docx')
print('switched to TableGrid style')
PYEOF
python3 fix_style.py 2>&1 && python3 skills/docx/scripts/validate.py output/governance-diligence-memo.docx 2>&1 && rm -f /tmp/governance-diligence-memo.pdf && timeout 120 soffice --headless --convert-to pdf --outdir /tmp output/governance-diligence-memo.docx 2>&1 | tail -1 && pdfinfo /tmp/governance-diligence-memo.pdf 2>/dev/null | grep Pages"}

from docx import Document
from docx.shared import Pt, Inches
from docx.oxml.ns import qn
from docx.oxml import OxmlElement

doc = Document()
sec = doc.sections[0]
sec.left_margin = Inches(0.9); sec.right_margin = Inches(0.9)
USABLE = int((sec.page_width.inches - sec.left_margin.inches - sec.right_margin.inches)*1440)

# Method A: python-docx native add_table
doc.add_heading('Method A: native add_table', level=2)
ta = doc.add_table(rows=2, cols=4)
ta.style = 'Table Grid'
hdr = ['Stockholder','Class','Shares','%']
for i,h in enumerate(hdr):
    ta.rows[0].cells[i].text = h
row = ['Ridgeline Ventures Fund II, LP','Series A Preferred','3,500,000','~10.3%']
for i,v in enumerate(row):
    ta.rows[1].cells[i].text = v
# set width
for c in ta.columns:
    c.width = Inches(USABLE/1440/4)

# Method B: manual XML fixed layout full width
doc.add_heading('Method B: manual XML fixed', level=2)
tb = doc.add_table(rows=2, cols=4)
tb.style = 'Table Grid'
for i,h in enumerate(hdr):
    tb.rows[0].cells[i].text = h
for i,v in enumerate(row):
    tb.rows[1].cells[i].text = v
tblPr = tb._tbl.tblPr
tw = OxmlElement('w:tblW'); tw.set(qn('w:w'), str(USABLE)); tw.set(qn('w:type'),'dxa'); tblPr.append(tw)
lay = OxmlElement('w:tblLayout'); lay.set(qn('w:type'),'fixed'); tblPr.append(lay)
grid = tb._tbl.find(qn('w:tblGrid'))
cols = grid.findall(qn('w:gridCol'))
w = USABLE//4
for c in cols:
    c.set(qn('w:w'), str(w))
for r in tb.rows:
    for cell in r.cells:
        tcPr = cell._tc.tcPr
        if tcPr is None:
            tcPr = OxmlElement('w:tcPr'); cell._tc.insert(0,tcPr)
        tcW = OxmlElement('w:tcW'); tcW.set(qn('w:w'), str(w)); tcW.set(qn('w:type'),'dxa'); tcPr.append(tcW)

doc.save('/tmp/test_tbl.docx')
print('saved test')

text: Both methods render multi-column in a clean test document — so my XML approach works. The problem is specific to the pandoc-generated file. I suspect out-of-order elements in `tblPr` (pandoc places `tblLook` before `tblCellMar`, violating the OOXML schema order, which can cause LibreOffice to misparse). Let me rebuild each table's `tblPr` in correct schema order.

tool_calls.name: bash
tool_calls.arguments:
{"command": "cd $WORKSPACE_DIR && cat > fix_order.py << 'PYEOF'
from docx import Document
from docx.oxml.ns import qn
from docx.oxml import OxmlElement
import copy

doc = Document('output/governance-diligence-memo.docx')
sec = doc.sections[0]
USABLE = int(round((sec.page_width.inches - sec.left_margin.inches - sec.right_margin.inches) * 1440))

def make(name, attrs=None):
    e = OxmlElement('w:'+name)
    if attrs:
        for k,v in attrs.items():
            e.set(qn('w:'+k), v)
    return e

for tbl in doc.tables:
    tblEl = tbl._tbl
    oldPr = tblEl.tblPr
    # preserve tblStyle val
    ts = oldPr.find(qn('w:tblStyle'))
    style_val = ts.get(qn('w:val')) if ts is not None else 'TableGrid'
    # preserve tblLook
    tl = oldPr.find(qn('w:tblLook'))
    look_xml = tl.xml if tl is not None else None

    # compute column widths
    grid = tblEl.find(qn('w:tblGrid'))
    cols = grid.findall(qn('w:gridCol'))
    widths = [int(c.get(qn('w:w')) or 100) for c in cols]
    s = sum(widths) or 1
    new = [max(1, int(round(w*USABLE/s))) for w in widths]
    if new:
        new[-1] = USABLE - sum(new[:-1])
    for c,w in zip(cols,new):
        c.set(qn('w:w'), str(max(1,w)))

    # rebuild tblPr in correct schema order
    newPr = OxmlElement('w:tblPr')
    newPr.append(make('tblStyle', {'val': style_val}))
    newPr.append(make('tblW', {'w': str(USABLE), 'type': 'dxa'}))
    newPr.append(make('jc', {'val': 'left'}))
    newPr.append(make('tblLayout', {'type': 'fixed'}))
    # tblCellMar
    mar = OxmlElement('w:tblCellMar')
    for side,val in (('top','18'),('bottom','18'),('start','54'),('end','54'),('left','54'),('right','54')):
        mar.append(make(side, {'w': val, 'type':'dxa'}))
    newPr.append(mar)
    # tblLook
    if look_xml:
        from lxml import etree
        newPr.append(etree.fromstring(look_xml))
    else:
        newPr.append(make('tblLook', {'val':'0020','firstRow':'1','lastRow':'0','firstColumn':'0','lastColumn':'0','noHBand':'0','noVBand':'0'}))
    # replace
    tblEl.replace(oldPr, newPr)

    # set tcW on every cell to match column width
    for row in tbl.rows:
        cells = row.cells
        for idx, cell in enumerate(cells):
            tcPr = cell._tc.tcPr
            if tcPr is None:
                tcPr = OxmlElement('w:tcPr'); cell._tc.insert(0, tcPr)
            # remove old tcW
            for old in tcPr.findall(qn('w:tcW')):
                tcPr.remove(old)
            tcW = make('tcW', {'w': str(max(1,new[idx] if idx<len(new) else USABLE//max(1,len(cells)))), 'type':'dxa'})
            # tcW should be early in tcPr; insert at position 0 after any tcStyle? just append is usually fine, but put first
            tcPr.insert(0, tcW)
            # tight paragraphs
            for p in cell.paragraphs:
                pf = p.paragraph_format
                pf.space_before=Pt(0) if False else None
        # cantSplit removal
        trPr = row._tr.trPr
        if trPr is not None:
            for cs in trPr.findall(qn('w:cantSplit')):
                trPr.remove(cs)

from docx.shared import Pt
doc.save('output/governance-diligence-memo.docx')
print('rebuilt tblPr in schema order; USABLE=',USABLE)
PYEOF
python3 fix_order.py 2>&1 && python3 skills/docx/scripts/validate.py output/governance-diligence-memo.docx 2>&1 && rm -f /tmp/governance-diligence-memo.pdf && timeout 120 soffice --headless --convert-to pdf --outdir /tmp output/governance-diligence-memo.docx 2>&1 | tail -1 && pdfinfo /tmp/governance-diligence-memo.pdf 2>/dev/null | grep Pages"}

{"file_path": "fix_order.py", "content": "from docx import Document
from docx.oxml.ns import qn
from docx.oxml import OxmlElement
from docx.shared import Pt
import lxml.etree as etree

doc = Document('output/governance-diligence-memo.docx')
sec = doc.sections[0]
USABLE = int(round((sec.page_width.inches - sec.left_margin.inches - sec.right_margin.inches) * 1440))

def make(name, attrs=None):
    e = OxmlElement('w:' + name)
    if attrs:
        for k, v in attrs.items():
            e.set(qn('w:' + k), v)
    return e

for tbl in doc.tables:
    tblEl = tbl._tbl
    oldPr = tblEl.tblPr
    ts = oldPr.find(qn('w:tblStyle'))
    style_val = ts.get(qn('w:val')) if ts is not None else 'TableGrid'
    tl = oldPr.find(qn('w:tblLook'))
    look_el = etree.fromstring(etree.tostring(tl)) if tl is not None else None

    grid = tblEl.find(qn('w:tblGrid'))
    cols = grid.findall(qn('w:gridCol'))
    widths = [int(c.get(qn('w:w')) or 100) for c in cols]
    s = sum(widths) or 1
    new = [max(1, int(round(w * USABLE / s))) for w in widths]
    if new:
        new[-1] = USABLE - sum(new[:-1])
    for c, w in zip(cols, new):
        c.set(qn('w:w'), str(max(1, w)))

    newPr = OxmlElement('w:tblPr')
    newPr.append(make('tblStyle', {'val': style_val}))
    newPr.append(make('tblW', {'w': str(USABLE), 'type': 'dxa'}))
    newPr.append(make('jc', {'val': 'left'}))
    newPr.append(make('tblLayout', {'type': 'fixed'}))
    mar = OxmlElement('w:tblCellMar')
    for side, val in (('top', '18'), ('bottom', '18'), ('start', '54'), ('end', '54'), ('left', '54'), ('right', '54')):
        mar.append(make(side, {'w': val, 'type': 'dxa'}))
    newPr.append(mar)
    if look_el is not None:
        newPr.append(look_el)
    else:
        newPr.append(make('tblLook', {'val': '0020', 'firstRow': '1', 'lastRow': '0', 'firstColumn': '0', 'lastColumn': '0', 'noHBand': '0', 'noVBand': '0'}))
    tblEl.replace(oldPr, newPr)

    for row in tbl.rows:
        cells = row.cells
        for idx, cell in enumerate(cells):
            tcPr = cell._tc.tcPr
            if tcPr is None:
                tcPr = OxmlElement('w:tcPr')
                cell._tc.insert(0, tcPr)
            for old in tcPr.findall(qn('w:tcW')):
                tcPr.remove(old)
            cw = new[idx] if idx < len(new) else USABLE // max(1, len(cells))
            tcW = make('tcW', {'w': str(max(1, cw)), 'type': 'dxa'})
            tcPr.insert(0, tcW)
            for p in cell.paragraphs:
                pf = p.paragraph_format
                pf.space_before = Pt(0)
                pf.space_after = Pt(0)
                pf.line_spacing = 1.0
                for r in p.runs:
                    r.font.size = Pt(8.5)
                    r.font.name = 'Calibri'
        trPr = row._tr.trPr
        if trPr is not None:
            for cs in trPr.findall(qn('w:cantSplit')):
                trPr.remove(cs)

doc.save('output/governance-diligence-memo.docx')
print('rebuilt tblPr in schema order; USABLE=', USABLE)"}
